question: Which customers have the highest spending on tracks from a single genre and what genre is it?
ddl_context:
## album
Music albums (records, releases). Each album belongs to one artist and contains multiple tracks.

Columns:
  - album_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "album_id" represents a unique identifier for each album in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - title (CHARACTER VARYING(160)): The "title" column represents the names of music albums.
    Examples: Heart of the Night, The Cream Of Clapton, Van Halen III
  - artist_id (INTEGER) -> artist.artist_id: The artist_id column represents a unique identifier for each artist associated with an album in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3

Relationships:
  - album -> artist (MANY_TO_ONE): album.artist_id = artist.artist_id
  - album -> track (ONE_TO_MANY): album.album_id = track.album_id

## artist
Musical artists, bands, and performers in the catalog.

Columns:
  - artist_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "artist_id" represents a unique identifier for each artist in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the names of musical artists or bands.
    Examples: Legião Urbana, Ben Harper, Incognito

Relationships:
  - artist -> album (ONE_TO_MANY): artist.artist_id = album.artist_id

## customer
Customers (buyers, accounts) who purchase music. Includes contact info, address, country. Can be assigned to a support rep employee.

Columns:
  - customer_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The "customer_id" column represents a unique identifier assigned to each customer in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - first_name (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "first_name" column represents the given names of customers, allowing for variable-length character entries up to 40 characters.
    Examples: Martha, Edward, Victor
  - last_name (CHARACTER VARYING(20)): The "last_name" column represents the family or surname of customers, allowing for a maximum of 20 characters.
    Examples: Sampaio, Muñoz, Wójcik
  - company (CHARACTER VARYING(80)): The "company" column represents the names of businesses or organizations associated with each customer.
    Examples: Apple Inc., Telus, Embraer - Empresa Brasileira de Aeronáutica S.A.
  - address (CHARACTER VARYING(70)): The "address" column represents the residential or business locations of customers, formatted as variable-length strings.
    Examples: 202 Hoxton Street, 3,Raj Bhavan Road, Rua dos Campeões Europeus de Viena, 4350
  - city (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "city" column represents the names of cities where customers reside.
    Examples: Budapest, Porto, Reno
  - state (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "state" column represents the abbreviated designations of geographical regions or states associated with customers.
    Examples: SP, RM, CA
  - country (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "country" column represents the country of residence for each customer, storing names of countries as variable-length strings.
    Examples: Argentina, Spain, Italy
  - postal_code (CHARACTER VARYING(10)): The "postal_code" column represents the mailing codes used to identify specific geographic areas for customer addresses, accommodating various formats including alphanumeric and numeric codes.
    Examples: K2P 1L7, X1A 1N6, 110017
  - phone (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "phone" column represents the contact telephone numbers of customers, formatted to include international dialing codes and varying local formats.
    Examples: +1 (617) 522-1333, +91 0124 39883988, +55 (21) 2271-7000
  - fax (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "fax" column represents the fax numbers of customers, formatted with international dialing codes and varying styles.
    Examples: +1 (780) 434-5565, +1 (408) 996-1011, +55 (21) 2271-7070
  - email (CHARACTER VARYING(60)): The "email" column represents the email addresses of customers, used for communication and account identification.
    Examples: ladislav_kovacs@apple.hu, johngordon22@yahoo.com, enrique_munoz@yahoo.es
  - support_rep_id (INTEGER) -> employee.employee_id: The `support_rep_id` column represents the unique identifier for the customer support representative assigned to assist each customer.
    Examples: 3, 5, 4
    Note: Internal customers have support_rep_id IS NOT NULL

Relationships:
  - customer -> invoice (ONE_TO_MANY): customer.customer_id = invoice.customer_id
  - customer -> employee (MANY_TO_ONE): customer.support_rep_id = employee.employee_id

## employee
Company employees (staff). Some are support reps (agents) assigned to customers. Includes hire date, title, manager (reports_to).

Columns:
  - employee_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The "employee_id" column represents a unique identifier assigned to each employee in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - last_name (CHARACTER VARYING(20)): The "last_name" column represents the family names of employees in the organization.
    Examples: Adams, King, Johnson
  - first_name (CHARACTER VARYING(20)): The "first_name" column represents the given names of employees, limited to a maximum of 20 characters.
    Examples: Nancy, Jane, Laura
  - title (CHARACTER VARYING(30)): The "title" column represents the job positions or roles held by employees within the organization.
    Examples: General Manager, Sales Support Agent, IT Manager
  - reports_to (INTEGER) -> employee.employee_id: The "reports_to" column represents the employee ID of the supervisor or manager to whom the employee directly reports.
    Examples: 1, 2, 6
    Note: NULL for top-level managers
  - birth_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The "birth_date" column represents the date and time of birth for each employee, stored as a timestamp without time zone information.
    Examples: 1958-12-08 00:00:00, 1962-02-18 00:00:00, 1973-08-29 00:00:00
  - hire_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The "hire_date" column represents the date and time when each employee was hired, recorded as a timestamp without time zone information.
    Examples: 2002-08-14 00:00:00, 2002-04-01 00:00:00, 2003-10-17 00:00:00
  - address (CHARACTER VARYING(70)): The "address" column represents the residential or work location of employees, formatted as variable-length strings containing street addresses.
    Examples: 5827 Bowness Road NW, 825 8 Ave SW, 1111 6 Ave SW
  - city (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "city" column represents the names of cities where employees are located or reside.
    Examples: Lethbridge, Edmonton, Calgary
  - state (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "state" column represents the abbreviation of the U.S. state where the employee is located or registered, as indicated by the sample value 'AB'.
    Examples: AB
  - country (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "country" column represents the country of residence or employment for each employee, stored as a variable-length string.
    Examples: Canada
  - postal_code (CHARACTER VARYING(10)): The "postal_code" column represents the postal codes of employees' addresses, formatted according to Canadian postal code standards.
    Examples: T2P 5M5, T3B 1Y7, T1K 5N8
  - phone (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "phone" column represents the contact telephone numbers of employees, formatted to include country codes and area codes.
    Examples: +1 (403) 467-3351, 1 (780) 836-9987, +1 (780) 428-9482
  - fax (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "fax" column represents the fax numbers of employees, formatted as strings that may include country codes and area codes.
    Examples: +1 (403) 456-8485, 1 (780) 836-9543, +1 (403) 263-4289
  - email (CHARACTER VARYING(60)): The "email" column represents the email addresses of employees within the organization.
    Examples: michael@chinookcorp.com, robert@chinookcorp.com, margaret@chinookcorp.com

Relationships:
  - employee -> employee (MANY_TO_ONE): employee.reports_to = manager.employee_id
  - employee -> customer (ONE_TO_MANY): employee.employee_id = customer.support_rep_id

## genre
Music genre classifications (Rock, Jazz, Classical, etc.).

Columns:
  - genre_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "genre_id" represents a unique identifier for each genre in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the various genres of music and entertainment, categorizing them by their specific styles or themes.
    Examples: Heavy Metal, TV Shows, Latin

Relationships:
  - genre -> track (ONE_TO_MANY): genre.genre_id = track.genre_id

## invoice
Sales invoices (orders, transactions, purchases). Contains total amount, billing address, and date. Use with invoice_line for revenue, spending, and sales analysis.

Columns:
  - invoice_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The `invoice_id` column represents a unique identifier for each invoice in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - customer_id (INTEGER) -> customer.customer_id: The "customer_id" column represents a unique identifier for each customer associated with an invoice.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - invoice_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The `invoice_date` column represents the date and time when each invoice was issued, recorded as a timestamp without time zone information.
    Examples: 2023-07-30 00:00:00, 2022-02-09 00:00:00, 2024-08-28 00:00:00
    Note: Dates range from 2009 to 2013. Fiscal year is Jan-Dec.
  - billing_address (CHARACTER VARYING(70)): The "billing_address" column represents the physical addresses where invoices are sent for billing purposes.
    Examples: 202 Hoxton Street, Rua dos Campeões Europeus de Viena, 4350, 3,Raj Bhavan Road
  - billing_city (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The `billing_city` column represents the names of cities where customers are billed for their invoices.
    Examples: Porto, Budapest, Reno
  - billing_state (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "billing_state" column represents the abbreviated state or province codes associated with the billing address of an invoice.
    Examples: SP, RM, CA
  - billing_country (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "billing_country" column represents the country associated with the billing address for each invoice.
    Examples: Argentina, Spain, Italy
  - billing_postal_code (CHARACTER VARYING(10)): The `billing_postal_code` column represents the postal or ZIP codes associated with billing addresses for invoices, accommodating various formats from different regions.
    Examples: K2P 1L7, X1A 1N6, 110017
  - total (NUMERIC): The "total" column represents the total monetary amount of each invoice, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 8.94, 9.91, 15.86
    Note: Sum of (unit_price * quantity) from invoice_line items

Relationships:
  - invoice -> customer (MANY_TO_ONE): invoice.customer_id = customer.customer_id
  - invoice -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): invoice.invoice_id = invoice_line.invoice_id

## invoice_line
Line items for invoices. Each row is a track purchase with unit_price and quantity. Join to invoice for revenue, sales totals, and spending by customer or region.

Columns:
  - invoice_line_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The `invoice_line_id` column represents a unique identifier for each line item in an invoice.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - invoice_id (INTEGER) -> invoice.invoice_id: The "invoice_id" column represents a unique identifier for each invoice associated with the corresponding line items in the invoice_line table.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - track_id (INTEGER) -> track.track_id: The "track_id" column represents a unique identifier for each music track associated with an invoice line item.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price per individual unit of a product or service listed in an invoice line, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
  - quantity (INTEGER): The "quantity" column represents the number of units of a specific item included in an invoice line.
    Examples: 1

Relationships:
  - invoice_line -> invoice (MANY_TO_ONE): invoice_line.invoice_id = invoice.invoice_id
  - invoice_line -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): invoice_line.track_id = track.track_id

## media_type
Audio file formats and encoding types (MPEG, AAC, Protected AAC, etc.).

Columns:
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The `media_type_id` column represents a unique identifier for different types of media in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the various types of media files, specifying their format and any associated attributes such as protection or purchase status.
    Examples: AAC audio file, Protected MPEG-4 video file, MPEG audio file

Relationships:
  - media_type -> track (ONE_TO_MANY): media_type.media_type_id = track.media_type_id

## playlist
User-created playlists (collections, mixtapes) grouping tracks together.

Columns:
  - playlist_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The `playlist_id` column represents a unique identifier for each playlist in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the titles or categories of playlists, indicating the type of content they contain, such as TV shows, movies, or music.
    Examples: TV Shows, Movies, Music Videos

Relationships:
  - playlist -> playlist_track (ONE_TO_MANY): playlist.playlist_id = playlist_track.playlist_id

## playlist_track
Junction table linking playlists to tracks. Many-to-many: a track can appear in multiple playlists.

Columns:
  - playlist_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> playlist.playlist_id: The `playlist_id` column represents the unique identifier for each playlist to which a track belongs.
    Examples: 5, 18, 16
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> track.track_id: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the playlist, with values indicating the specific tracks associated with the playlist.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3

Relationships:
  - playlist_track -> playlist (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.playlist_id = playlist.playlist_id
  - playlist_track -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.track_id = track.track_id

## track
Individual songs (tracks, recordings). Includes name, duration (milliseconds), size (bytes), unit_price, composer. Belongs to an album, genre, and media_type.

Columns:
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(200)): The "name" column represents the titles of music tracks in the database.
    Examples: Anyway, Anyhow, Anywhere, Não Sou Mais Disso, I Still Haven't Found What I'm Looking for
  - album_id (INTEGER) -> album.album_id: The album_id column represents the unique identifier for the album to which each track belongs.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) -> media_type.media_type_id: The `media_type_id` column represents the unique identifier for different types of media associated with each track, such as audio files or video files.
    Examples: 1, 3, 5
  - genre_id (INTEGER) -> genre.genre_id: The `genre_id` column represents a unique identifier for different music genres associated with each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - composer (CHARACTER VARYING(220)): The "composer" column represents the names of individuals or groups who composed the musical tracks, with multiple composers listed for some entries.
    Examples: Aldir Blanc/Jayme Vignoli, Alex Van Halen/David Lee Roth/Edward Van Halen/Michael Anthony, Grazielle/Roque Ferreira
  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.
    Examples: 407980, 264829, 464039
    Note: 5 minutes = 300,000 milliseconds
  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
    Note: Standard price 0.99, premium/long tracks 1.99

Relationships:
  - track -> album (MANY_TO_ONE): track.album_id = album.album_id
  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id
  - track -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): track.track_id = invoice_line.track_id

  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.

  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.

## track
Individual songs (tracks, recordings). Includes name, duration (milliseconds), size (bytes), unit_price, composer. Belongs to an album, genre, and media_type.

Columns:
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(200)): The "name" column represents the titles of music tracks in the database.
    Examples: Anyway, Anyhow, Anywhere, Não Sou Mais Disso, I Still Haven't Found What I'm Looking for
  - album_id (INTEGER) -> album.album_id: The album_id column represents the unique identifier for the album to which each track belongs.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) -> media_type.media_type_id: The `media_type_id` column represents the unique identifier for different types of media associated with each track, such as audio files or video files.
    Examples: 1, 3, 5
  - genre_id (INTEGER) -> genre.genre_id: The `genre_id` column represents a unique identifier for different music genres associated with each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - composer (CHARACTER VARYING(220)): The "composer" column represents the names of individuals or groups who composed the musical tracks, with multiple composers listed for some entries.
    Examples: Aldir Blanc/Jayme Vignoli, Alex Van Halen/David Lee Roth/Edward Van Halen/Michael Anthony, Grazielle/Roque Ferreira
  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.
    Examples: 407980, 264829, 464039
    Note: 5 minutes = 300,000 milliseconds
  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
    Note: Standard price 0.99, premium/long tracks 1.99

Relationships:

  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id
  - track -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): track.track_id = invoice_line.track_id

  - birth_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The "birth_date" column represents the date and time of birth for each employee, stored as a timestamp without time zone information.
    Examples: 1958-12-08 00:00:00, 1962-02-18 00:00:00, 1973-08-29 00:00:00
  - hire_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The "hire_date" column represents the date and time when each employee was hired, recorded as a timestamp without time zone information.
    Examples: 2002-08-14 00:00:00, 2002-04-01 00:00:00, 2003-10-17 00:00:00
  - address (CHARACTER VARYING(70)): The "address" column represents the residential or work location of employees, formatted as variable-length strings containing street addresses.

  - city (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "city" column represents the names of cities where employees are located or reside.
    Examples: Lethbridge, Edmonton, Calgary
  - state (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "state" column represents the abbreviation of the U.S. state where the employee is located or registered, as indicated by the sample value 'AB'.
    Examples: AB
  - country (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "country" column represents the country of residence or employment for each employee, stored as a variable-length string.
    Examples: Canada
  - postal_code (CHARACTER VARYING(10)): The "postal_code" column represents the postal codes of employees' addresses, formatted according to Canadian postal code standards.
    Examples: T2P 5M5, T3B 1Y7, T1K 5N8
  - phone (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "phone" column represents the contact telephone numbers of employees, formatted to include country codes and area codes.
    Examples: +1 (403) 467-3351, 1 (780) 836-9987, +1 (780) 428-9482
  - fax (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "fax" column represents the fax numbers of employees, formatted as strings that may include country codes and area codes.
    Examples: +1 (403) 456-8485, 1 (780) 836-9543, +1 (403) 263-4289
  - email (CHARACTER VARYING(60)): The "email" column represents the email addresses of employees within the organization.

## invoice
Sales invoices (orders, transactions, purchases). Contains total amount, billing address, and date. Use with invoice_line for revenue, spending, and sales analysis.

Columns:

    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - customer_id (INTEGER) -> customer.customer_id: The "customer_id" column represents a unique identifier for each customer associated with an invoice.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - invoice_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The `invoice_date` column represents the date and time when each invoice was issued, recorded as a timestamp without time zone information.
    Examples: 2023-07-30 00:00:00, 2022-02-09 00:00:00, 2024-08-28 00:00:00
    Note: Dates range from 2009 to 2013. Fiscal year is Jan-Dec.
  - billing_address (CHARACTER VARYING(70)): The "billing_address" column represents the physical addresses where invoices are sent for billing purposes.
    Examples: 202 Hoxton Street, Rua dos Campeões Europeus de Viena, 4350, 3,Raj Bhavan Road
  - billing_city (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The `billing_city` column represents the names of cities where customers are billed for their invoices.
    Examples: Porto, Budapest, Reno
  - billing_state (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "billing_state" column represents the abbreviated state or province codes associated with the billing address of an invoice.
    Examples: SP, RM, CA
  - billing_country (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The "billing_country" column represents the country associated with the billing address for each invoice.
    Examples: Argentina, Spain, Italy
  - billing_postal_code (CHARACTER VARYING(10)): The `billing_postal_code` column represents the postal or ZIP codes associated with billing addresses for invoices, accommodating various formats from different regions.

## track
Individual songs (tracks, recordings). Includes name, duration (milliseconds), size (bytes), unit_price, composer. Belongs to an album, genre, and media_type.

Columns:
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(200)): The "name" column represents the titles of music tracks in the database.
    Examples: Anyway, Anyhow, Anywhere, Não Sou Mais Disso, I Still Haven't Found What I'm Looking for
  - album_id (INTEGER) -> album.album_id: The album_id column represents the unique identifier for the album to which each track belongs.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) -> media_type.media_type_id: The `media_type_id` column represents the unique identifier for different types of media associated with each track, such as audio files or video files.

  - genre_id (INTEGER) -> genre.genre_id: The `genre_id` column represents a unique identifier for different music genres associated with each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3

  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.
    Examples: 407980, 264829, 464039

  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
    Note: Standard price 0.99, premium/long tracks 1.99

  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id
  - track -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): track.track_id = invoice_line.track_id

    Examples: 1, 2, 3

Junction table linking playlists to tracks. Many-to-many: a track can appear in multiple playlists.

    Examples: 5, 18, 16
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> track.track_id: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the playlist, with values indicating the specific tracks associated with the playlist.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3

  - playlist_track -> playlist (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.playlist_id = playlist.playlist_id
  - playlist_track -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.track_id = track.track_id

## track
Individual songs (tracks, recordings). Includes name, duration (milliseconds), size (bytes), unit_price, composer. Belongs to an album, genre, and media_type.

Columns:
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(200)): The "name" column represents the titles of music tracks in the database.
    Examples: Anyway, Anyhow, Anywhere, Não Sou Mais Disso, I Still Haven't Found What I'm Looking for
  - album_id (INTEGER) -> album.album_id: The album_id column represents the unique identifier for the album to which each track belongs.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) -> media_type.media_type_id: The `media_type_id` column represents the unique identifier for different types of media associated with each track, such as audio files or video files.
    Examples: 1, 3, 5
  - genre_id (INTEGER) -> genre.genre_id: The `genre_id` column represents a unique identifier for different music genres associated with each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - composer (CHARACTER VARYING(220)): The "composer" column represents the names of individuals or groups who composed the musical tracks, with multiple composers listed for some entries.
    Examples: Aldir Blanc/Jayme Vignoli, Alex Van Halen/David Lee Roth/Edward Van Halen/Michael Anthony, Grazielle/Roque Ferreira
  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.
    Examples: 407980, 264829, 464039
    Note: 5 minutes = 300,000 milliseconds
  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
    Note: Standard price 0.99, premium/long tracks 1.99

Relationships:
  - track -> album (MANY_TO_ONE): track.album_id = album.album_id
  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id
  - track -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): track.track_id = invoice_line.track_id

Columns:
  - playlist_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The `playlist_id` column represents a unique identifier for each playlist in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the titles or categories of playlists, indicating the type of content they contain, such as TV shows, movies, or music.
    Examples: TV Shows, Movies, Music Videos

Relationships:
  - playlist -> playlist_track (ONE_TO_MANY): playlist.playlist_id = playlist_track.playlist_id

## playlist_track
Junction table linking playlists to tracks. Many-to-many: a track can appear in multiple playlists.

Columns:
  - playlist_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> playlist.playlist_id: The `playlist_id` column represents the unique identifier for each playlist to which a track belongs.
    Examples: 5, 18, 16
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> track.track_id: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the playlist, with values indicating the specific tracks associated with the playlist.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3

Relationships:
  - playlist_track -> playlist (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.playlist_id = playlist.playlist_id
  - playlist_track -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.track_id = track.track_id

## track
Individual songs (tracks, recordings). Includes name, duration (milliseconds), size (bytes), unit_price, composer. Belongs to an album, genre, and media_type.

Columns:
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(200)): The "name" column represents the titles of music tracks in the database.
    Examples: Anyway, Anyhow, Anywhere, Não Sou Mais Disso, I Still Haven't Found What I'm Looking for
  - album_id (INTEGER) -> album.album_id: The album_id column represents the unique identifier for the album to which each track belongs.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) -> media_type.media_type_id: The `media_type_id` column represents the unique identifier for different types of media associated with each track, such as audio files or video files.
    Examples: 1, 3, 5
  - genre_id (INTEGER) -> genre.genre_id: The `genre_id` column represents a unique identifier for different music genres associated with each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - composer (CHARACTER VARYING(220)): The "composer" column represents the names of individuals or groups who composed the musical tracks, with multiple composers listed for some entries.
    Examples: Aldir Blanc/Jayme Vignoli, Alex Van Halen/David Lee Roth/Edward Van Halen/Michael Anthony, Grazielle/Roque Ferreira
  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.
    Examples: 407980, 264829, 464039
    Note: 5 minutes = 300,000 milliseconds
  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
    Note: Standard price 0.99, premium/long tracks 1.99

Relationships:
  - track -> album (MANY_TO_ONE): track.album_id = album.album_id
  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id
  - track -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): track.track_id = invoice_line.track_id

  - fax (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "fax" column represents the fax numbers of customers, formatted with international dialing codes and varying styles.

  - email (CHARACTER VARYING(60)): The "email" column represents the email addresses of customers, used for communication and account identification.
    Examples: ladislav_kovacs@apple.hu, johngordon22@yahoo.com, enrique_munoz@yahoo.es
  - support_rep_id (INTEGER) -> employee.employee_id: The `support_rep_id` column represents the unique identifier for the customer support representative assigned to assist each customer.
    Examples: 3, 5, 4

Relationships:

  - customer -> employee (MANY_TO_ONE): customer.support_rep_id = employee.employee_id

  - hire_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The "hire_date" column represents the date and time when each employee was hired, recorded as a timestamp without time zone information.

  - fax (CHARACTER VARYING(24)): The "fax" column represents the fax numbers of employees, formatted as strings that may include country codes and area codes.

  - billing_postal_code (CHARACTER VARYING(10)): The `billing_postal_code` column represents the postal or ZIP codes associated with billing addresses for invoices, accommodating various formats from different regions.

    Examples: 1, 2, 3

  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.

  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.

  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id

  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.

  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.

    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - customer_id (INTEGER) -> customer.customer_id: The "customer_id" column represents a unique identifier for each customer associated with an invoice.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - invoice_date (TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE): The `invoice_date` column represents the date and time when each invoice was issued, recorded as a timestamp without time zone information.
    Examples: 2023-07-30 00:00:00, 2022-02-09 00:00:00, 2024-08-28 00:00:00

  - billing_city (CHARACTER VARYING(40)): The `billing_city` column represents the names of cities where customers are billed for their invoices.

    Examples: Argentina, Spain, Italy
  - billing_postal_code (CHARACTER VARYING(10)): The `billing_postal_code` column represents the postal or ZIP codes associated with billing addresses for invoices, accommodating various formats from different regions.

    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - track_id (INTEGER) -> track.track_id: The "track_id" column represents a unique identifier for each music track associated with an invoice line item.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price per individual unit of a product or service listed in an invoice line, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
  - quantity (INTEGER): The "quantity" column represents the number of units of a specific item included in an invoice line.
    Examples: 1

Relationships:
  - invoice_line -> invoice (MANY_TO_ONE): invoice_line.invoice_id = invoice.invoice_id
  - invoice_line -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): invoice_line.track_id = track.track_id

  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the various types of media files, specifying their format and any associated attributes such as protection or purchase status.

  - playlist -> playlist_track (ONE_TO_MANY): playlist.playlist_id = playlist_track.playlist_id

  - playlist_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> playlist.playlist_id: The `playlist_id` column represents the unique identifier for each playlist to which a track belongs.
    Examples: 5, 18, 16
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> track.track_id: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the playlist, with values indicating the specific tracks associated with the playlist.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3

Relationships:
  - playlist_track -> playlist (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.playlist_id = playlist.playlist_id
  - playlist_track -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.track_id = track.track_id

## track
Individual songs (tracks, recordings). Includes name, duration (milliseconds), size (bytes), unit_price, composer. Belongs to an album, genre, and media_type.

Columns:
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(200)): The "name" column represents the titles of music tracks in the database.
    Examples: Anyway, Anyhow, Anywhere, Não Sou Mais Disso, I Still Haven't Found What I'm Looking for
  - album_id (INTEGER) -> album.album_id: The album_id column represents the unique identifier for the album to which each track belongs.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) -> media_type.media_type_id: The `media_type_id` column represents the unique identifier for different types of media associated with each track, such as audio files or video files.
    Examples: 1, 3, 5
  - genre_id (INTEGER) -> genre.genre_id: The `genre_id` column represents a unique identifier for different music genres associated with each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - composer (CHARACTER VARYING(220)): The "composer" column represents the names of individuals or groups who composed the musical tracks, with multiple composers listed for some entries.
    Examples: Aldir Blanc/Jayme Vignoli, Alex Van Halen/David Lee Roth/Edward Van Halen/Michael Anthony, Grazielle/Roque Ferreira
  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.
    Examples: 407980, 264829, 464039
    Note: 5 minutes = 300,000 milliseconds
  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
    Note: Standard price 0.99, premium/long tracks 1.99

Relationships:
  - track -> album (MANY_TO_ONE): track.album_id = album.album_id
  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id
  - track -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): track.track_id = invoice_line.track_id

  - invoice_id (INTEGER) -> invoice.invoice_id: The "invoice_id" column represents a unique identifier for each invoice associated with the corresponding line items in the invoice_line table.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - track_id (INTEGER) -> track.track_id: The "track_id" column represents a unique identifier for each music track associated with an invoice line item.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price per individual unit of a product or service listed in an invoice line, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
  - quantity (INTEGER): The "quantity" column represents the number of units of a specific item included in an invoice line.
    Examples: 1

Relationships:
  - invoice_line -> invoice (MANY_TO_ONE): invoice_line.invoice_id = invoice.invoice_id
  - invoice_line -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): invoice_line.track_id = track.track_id

## media_type
Audio file formats and encoding types (MPEG, AAC, Protected AAC, etc.).

Columns:
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The `media_type_id` column represents a unique identifier for different types of media in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the various types of media files, specifying their format and any associated attributes such as protection or purchase status.
    Examples: AAC audio file, Protected MPEG-4 video file, MPEG audio file

Relationships:
  - media_type -> track (ONE_TO_MANY): media_type.media_type_id = track.media_type_id

## playlist
User-created playlists (collections, mixtapes) grouping tracks together.

Columns:
  - playlist_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The `playlist_id` column represents a unique identifier for each playlist in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(120)): The "name" column represents the titles or categories of playlists, indicating the type of content they contain, such as TV shows, movies, or music.
    Examples: TV Shows, Movies, Music Videos

Relationships:
  - playlist -> playlist_track (ONE_TO_MANY): playlist.playlist_id = playlist_track.playlist_id

## playlist_track
Junction table linking playlists to tracks. Many-to-many: a track can appear in multiple playlists.

Columns:
  - playlist_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> playlist.playlist_id: The `playlist_id` column represents the unique identifier for each playlist to which a track belongs.
    Examples: 5, 18, 16
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK] -> track.track_id: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the playlist, with values indicating the specific tracks associated with the playlist.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3

Relationships:
  - playlist_track -> playlist (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.playlist_id = playlist.playlist_id
  - playlist_track -> track (MANY_TO_ONE): playlist_track.track_id = track.track_id

## track
Individual songs (tracks, recordings). Includes name, duration (milliseconds), size (bytes), unit_price, composer. Belongs to an album, genre, and media_type.

Columns:
  - track_id (INTEGER) [PK]: The column "track_id" represents a unique identifier for each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - name (CHARACTER VARYING(200)): The "name" column represents the titles of music tracks in the database.
    Examples: Anyway, Anyhow, Anywhere, Não Sou Mais Disso, I Still Haven't Found What I'm Looking for
  - album_id (INTEGER) -> album.album_id: The album_id column represents the unique identifier for the album to which each track belongs.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - media_type_id (INTEGER) -> media_type.media_type_id: The `media_type_id` column represents the unique identifier for different types of media associated with each track, such as audio files or video files.
    Examples: 1, 3, 5
  - genre_id (INTEGER) -> genre.genre_id: The `genre_id` column represents a unique identifier for different music genres associated with each track in the database.
    Examples: 1, 2, 3
  - composer (CHARACTER VARYING(220)): The "composer" column represents the names of individuals or groups who composed the musical tracks, with multiple composers listed for some entries.
    Examples: Aldir Blanc/Jayme Vignoli, Alex Van Halen/David Lee Roth/Edward Van Halen/Michael Anthony, Grazielle/Roque Ferreira
  - milliseconds (INTEGER): The "milliseconds" column represents the duration of each track in milliseconds.
    Examples: 407980, 264829, 464039
    Note: 5 minutes = 300,000 milliseconds
  - bytes (INTEGER): The "bytes" column represents the size of each track in bytes.
    Examples: 7366142, 8044864, 7032960
  - unit_price (NUMERIC): The "unit_price" column represents the price of a single unit of a track, expressed as a numeric value.
    Examples: 1.99, 0.99
    Note: Standard price 0.99, premium/long tracks 1.99

Relationships:
  - track -> album (MANY_TO_ONE): track.album_id = album.album_id
  - track -> genre (MANY_TO_ONE): track.genre_id = genre.genre_id
  - track -> invoice_line (ONE_TO_MANY): track.track_id = invoice_line.track_id
